transcript:
DR: Mrs. Casas! Come in, come in. How is the garden doing this summer?
PT: Doctor, the tomatoes are showing off. I brought a little bag for the front desk girls — don't worry, I washed them. The zucchini, on the other hand, I am at war with. You turn your back one day and it's the size of a baseball bat.
DR: You're single-handedly feeding this clinic. It's wonderful to see you. So today is your yearly check — the full once-over. How have you been feeling since last summer?
PT: Thanks be to God, mostly good. I keep busy. Five of us in the house now — my daughter and the grandchildren moved in with me last year, you remember. She works nights at the hospital laundry, so I run the mornings. Lunches, school drop-off, the whole circus.
DR: That's a full-time job in itself. Are you getting any time for yourself? Friends, church, neighbors?
PT: Ah. Less than I should. By the time the little ones are asleep, I am also asleep. My friend Rosa calls, but visiting — no, not so much anymore. My world got small, Doctor. Busy, but small.
DR: I hear you, and I want to keep an eye on that — a busy house and a small world can still be a lonely combination. How is the knee treating you? The arthritis.
PT: Today, quiet. Zero complaints, if you can believe it. Summer is my friend — it's the winter when it creaks like my kitchen door. When it acts up I take the naproxen, the little blue ones from the pharmacy, 220, one with breakfast, and that settles it. Some weeks I take none at all.
DR: That's exactly how I like to hear it used — as needed, with food. No stomach burning, no black stools, no swelling in the knee?
PT: No, no, and no. My stomach is made of old country material. Very durable.
DR: Any chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations?
PT: No. I walk to the market and back, fifteen minutes each way, no problem. Uphill on the way home, I will add, for the record.
DR: Noted for the record. Any dizziness, headaches, changes in your vision, numbness?
PT: Nothing like that.
DR: How's your sleep? And your appetite?
PT: I sleep like a stone until five, then the worrying shift begins. Lying there doing arithmetic — the bills, my daughter, my sister back home. Twenty minutes of nonsense, then I get up and make coffee and it goes away.
DR: Let me ask the mood questions properly, I ask everyone. In the past two weeks, little interest or pleasure in things you usually enjoy?
PT: No, not at all. The garden alone keeps me in business.
DR: Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless?
PT: No. Tired sometimes, but not sad-tired. Just tired-tired.
DR: And the worry — nervous, anxious, on edge?
PT: A few days, yes. The five a.m. arithmetic.
DR: Trouble relaxing, or feeling something awful might happen?
PT: Once in a while. When my sister doesn't answer the phone for two days, my head writes a whole telenovela. Then she calls and it's cancelled.
DR: That puts your anxiety score at a five — mild territory. Worry that visits and leaves, like you say. If it ever starts staying — keeping you from sleep past those twenty minutes, or following you through the day — you tell me, yes?
PT: Yes, yes. I promise.
DR: Now, a few questions I ask every patient every year, and I ask them privately because they matter. Is anyone in your life humiliating you, frightening you, hurting you, or forcing you in any way?
PT: No, Doctor. And I know why you ask me especially. That was my first husband, many years ago, another lifetime. He is long gone from my life. In my house now it is me, my daughter, and three noisy angels. I am safe. I promise you that.
DR: Thank you for answering it straight — I'll keep asking every year all the same, because that's the job.
PT: Ask away. The answer will stay boring, God willing.
DR: Any cigarettes? I know the answer, but for the record.
PT: Ha! Two years of smoking when I was a teenager, to look interesting. It didn't work, so I quit. That was almost fifty years ago.
DR: Longest-standing quit in my whole panel. Alcohol? Any drugs, marijuana, pills beyond what we know about?
PT: A little cup of wine at Christmas. Otherwise no, nothing, never.
DR: That drug screen is a zero, as always. Now — medications. The list says only the naproxen, the 220 milligram tablets, as needed for the knee. Anything else at home? Vitamins, teas, anything from the botánica?
PT: Manzanilla tea, but that's for the soul, not the body. Otherwise just the naproxen. That's all.
DR: Reconciled and official. Let's look at your numbers, because you earned some good ones. Blood pressure today: 135 over 82. The top number is a touch above perfect — not medicine territory, but we watch it. Heart rate 60 and steady, breathing 12, and your weight is 73.8 kilos.
PT: Down! I was 78 once upon a time in that chart, I remember it.
DR: Down indeed — your BMI is 27.7 now. The market walks and the war with the zucchini are working. Now the blood tests from this morning. Your sugar average — the A1c — is 6.11 percent. Still prediabetes, same neighborhood as last year, holding steady. Today's sugar was 79, which is lovely. Kidneys perfect — creatinine 0.68. Sodium 138, potassium 3.9.
PT: And the cholesterol? That's my inheritance, my mother had it too.
DR: Your inheritance is behaving. Total cholesterol 168, the bad LDL is 87, the good HDL is 59, triglycerides 110. For someone with hyperlipidemia on the books, these are genuinely good numbers.
PT: The oatmeal gets the credit. And less rice than before — my daughter measures it now like a pharmacist.
DR: Keep the pharmacist on staff. Blood count: hemoglobin 16.9, hematocrit 47.6 — no sign of the old anemia today, your iron story looks closed. Platelets 351, white count 7.4, all in order.
PT: So the blood is rich again. Good. I told you the lentils would do it.
DR: You did tell me. Now open wide for me — I want to check your mouth. Mm. Your gums are puffy along the bottom, and they bleed a little when I touch them. Do they bleed when you brush?
PT: A little pink when I spit, yes. I thought that was normal at my age.
DR: It's common, not normal — it's gingivitis, the early, fixable kind of gum disease. I'm sending you to the dentist for a proper cleaning.
PT: Ay, the dentist. Fine. But I want it known I go under protest.
DR: Duly recorded. Brush twice a day, soft brush, and don't stop when it bleeds — that part gets better in a couple of weeks. Now, one more thing: your flu shot. I know it's only August —
PT: Already? The children aren't even back in school!
DR: You're here, the vaccine is here, and the calendar has a way of eating good intentions. Same one as every year, the trivalent.
PT: Fine, fine. Left arm. The right one stirs the pots.
DR: Perfect. So, the summary, Mrs. Casas: sugar — steady, keep the walks and the measured rice, we recheck next year. Cholesterol — excellent, keep the oatmeal. Knee — naproxen as needed with food is exactly right. Weight — moving the right way, well done. Mood — mild five a.m. worries, we keep an ear on it. Gums — dentist, under protest. Flu shot today. Questions for me?
PT: Only one. The grandchildren bring home every germ ever invented. The flu shot covers me for their whole plague season?
DR: That's exactly why we're doing it today — grandmother of three is a frontline occupation. Wash the hands, get the sleep, and you're as covered as I can make you.
PT: Then jab me and I'll go liberate my tomatoes from the waiting room.
NURSE: I heard there are tomatoes involved. Left arm? Little pinch... there, done. Pressure for a minute, and thank you for the bag, Mrs. Casas — you're everyone's favorite Tuesday.
PT: Flattery accepted. Same time next year, Doctor — God willing and the zucchini permitting.
note:
**Subjective:**
Eva Casas is a 62-year-old woman presenting for her annual general examination. She feels well overall. Her known osteoarthritis of the knee is currently asymptomatic (pain 0/10 today); she reports a winter-predominant pattern managed with over-the-counter naproxen as needed with food, without dyspepsia, melena, or knee swelling. She walks to the market daily (~30 minutes round trip). She denies chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, dizziness, headaches, visual changes, and focal numbness or weakness. Sleep is preserved except for brief early-morning rumination about finances and family; she endorses intermittent mild worry but no low mood, anhedonia, or hopelessness. She lives with her daughter and three grandchildren and provides substantial childcare; she acknowledges reduced peer contact, consistent with her documented limited social contact and social isolation. Given her remote history as a victim of intimate partner abuse, a private safety screen was conducted: she reports the relationship ended many years ago and states she feels safe at home. She is a refugee who resettled decades ago. Ex-smoker (brief use in adolescence, quit ~age 16); rare alcohol; denies drug use. Medications: naproxen sodium 220 mg oral tablet as needed. No known allergies.

**Objective:**
Vitals: BP 135/82 mmHg, HR 60/min, RR 12/min, weight 73.8 kg, height 163.2 cm, BMI 27.71 kg/m2, pain score 0/10.
Exam: Well-appearing, animated, in no distress. Heart regular without murmur; lungs clear. Knees without effusion or warmth; gait normal. Oral exam: edematous gingival margins with contact bleeding.
Labs: A1c 6.11%; glucose 79.11 mg/dL; BUN 7.7 mg/dL; creatinine 0.68 mg/dL; calcium 9.23 mg/dL; sodium 138.23 mmol/L; potassium 3.94 mmol/L; chloride 107.69 mmol/L; CO2 23.26 mmol/L. Lipids: total cholesterol 168.51 mg/dL, LDL (direct) 87.17 mg/dL, HDL 59.18 mg/dL, triglycerides 110.82 mg/dL. CBC: WBC 7.42, hemoglobin 16.89 g/dL, hematocrit 47.59%, MCV 82.03 fL, platelets 351.89.
Screenings: PHQ-2 total 0; GAD-7 total 5; HARK total 0; DAST-10 total 0.

**Assessment and Plan:**

### Prediabetes
A1c 6.11%, stable versus prior; fasting glucose normal at 79.11 mg/dL.
- Continue dietary portion control and daily walking; reinforce 5% weight-loss trajectory.
- Repeat A1c at next annual visit.

### Hyperlipidemia
Well-controlled panel today (LDL 87.17, HDL 59.18 mg/dL) on lifestyle measures.
- Continue current diet; repeat lipid panel annually.

### Osteoarthritis of knee
Asymptomatic today with a winter-predominant flare pattern.
- Continue naproxen sodium 220 mg as needed with food; reviewed GI precautions.
- Encourage continued walking and quadriceps activity.

### Stress and mild anxiety symptoms
GAD-7 score 5 with early-morning rumination; PHQ-2 score 0. New stress documented in the context of caregiving load and social isolation.
- Supportive counseling provided; encouraged scheduled peer contact.
- Reassess mood and anxiety screens at next visit; return sooner if symptoms escalate.

### Gingivitis
Gingival edema with contact bleeding on exam.
- Patient referral for dental care placed for cleaning and examination; oral hygiene counseled.

### Safety screening (history of intimate partner abuse)
Private HARK screen negative (score 0); patient reports feeling safe at home.
- Continue routine annual screening; resources available on request.

### Anemia (historical)
Hemoglobin 16.89 g/dL and hematocrit 47.59% today without anemia.
- No intervention; continue routine monitoring.

### Preventive care
Depression and drug screens negative; ex-smoker, no current tobacco.
- Influenza vaccine (split virus, trivalent, preservative-free) administered today.
- Medication reconciliation and health and social care needs assessment completed; return in one year.
